preFix.title: Managing a legacy Feed-in Tariff system
preFix.subtitle: What happens to your FiT payments, export meter, and system when the tariff eventually ends
preFix.excerpt: The UK Feed-in Tariff scheme closed to new applicants in March 2019. Existing installations continue to receive payments for 20 years from commissioning, with the final FiT contracts expiring from 2039 onward. If your system is on the FiT, understanding the payment structure, the export meter, and the options when the tariff ends lets you maximise the remaining value and plan the transition.
preFix.sourceNotes: Ofgem FiT scheme guidance and FAQ 2024. Energy Saving Trust FiT explanation for existing customers. Ofgem Smart Export Guarantee (SEG) registered suppliers list 2025.

content.text: The Feed-in Tariff pays a generation tariff on every kWh your system generates. No matter whether you use it or export it. For a system commissioned in 2013 at a generation rate of 14.90p/kWh. A 3.5 kWp roof producing 3,000 kWh/year receives substantial annual generation payments. The scheme also pays an export tariff on either actual or deemed export.
FiT payments come from the licensed supplier that runs your tariff. This is usually your energy retailer, or a dedicated FiT administrator if you have switched. Two payment streams:.

Generation payment: based on the generation meter reading submitted to your FiT administrator, typically quarterly or annually. The payment rate is the rate set when you commissioned, indexed to RPI each year.
Export payment: either a deemed 50% of generation or an actual reading from an export meter. Deemed export is paid automatically; actual export requires submitting an export meter reading.
Keep submitting readings on time. FiT payments cannot be backdated beyond the current period. Missing a quarterly submission means losing that quarter's payment permanently.

Self-consume more. The generation tariff is paid no matter what you do with the electricity. But for every kWh you shift from grid import to self-consumption. You also save 27p/kWh on your electricity bill, far more than the 5p/kWh export rate. Running dishwashers, washing machines, and EV charging during daylight hours maximises this.

Check whether an export meter installation makes sense. If your panels consistently produce more than you consume and you are on deemed export. An actual export meter could capture more than the deemed 50%. The economics depend on your self-consumption ratio and generation rate.

When your 20-year contract expires (for a 2010 installation. That is 2030; for a 2019 installation, 2039), you can switch to the SEG for export payments. SEG rates in 2025 range from 5p/kWh to 15p/kWh. The generation tariff disappears at the end of the FiT contract, the system continues to generate freely. But there is no further payment for generation. At that point, adding battery storage or a V2G system becomes more attractive. Because the priority shifts entirely from generation payments to maximising self-consumption.
